claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 10 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 2 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 2 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 8 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 10 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Child is not fostered
Child does not work 24+ hours/week
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's start the eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is the child (or children) you want to claim Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know the age(s) of the child(ren) you're asking about, and if you have multiple children, please share their names and ages so I can assess eligibility for each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi there! I'm looking to claim Child Benefit and I have three children I'd like to ask about.

I have Alex who's 10 years old, Blake who's 19, and Charlie who's 2 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! Thank you for providing that information. Let me assess eligibility for each of your three children: Alex (10), Blake (19), and Charlie (2).

Let's start working through the requirements for each child.

---

**For Alex (10) and Charlie (2):**

Since they are both **under 16 years old**, they automatically meet the age requirement. Let me check the next requirement for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For children under 16, this question about working hours is relevant. 

**For Alex (10) and Charlie (2):** Are either of them working 24 hours or more per week in paid employment? (This would be unusual for children this young, but I need to confirm.)

---

**For Blake (19):**

Since Blake is **16 to 19 years old**, I need to check their education or training status.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Let me get the correct node for Blake's situation:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (19):**

Since Blake is 19, I need to know about their education or training status. Is Blake:

1. **Currently in approved education or training** (e.g., A-levels, T-levels, NVQ, apprenticeship)?
2. **Recently left education or training** (within the last 20 weeks)?
3. **Neither** - not in education/training and didn't recently leave?

---

Please let me know:
1. **For Alex and Charlie:** Are they working 24+ hours per week? (I assume not, given their ages)
2. **For Blake:** What is their current education/training status?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh no, Alex and Charlie definitely aren't working - they're far too young for that!

As for Blake, he's not in any education or training at the moment. He finished school a while back and hasn't gone on to do anything since then.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that!

---

**For Alex (10) and Charlie (2):**

Great, since they're not working, they pass this check. Let me continue with the next requirement for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (10) and Charlie (2):**

Next question: Are Alex or Charlie receiving any benefits in their own right, such as **Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)** or **Universal Credit**? (Again, this would be very unusual for children their ages.)

---

**For Blake (19):**

Since Blake finished school "a while back" and isn't in education or training, I need to check if he left recently enough to qualify for an extension period.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see that the extension period is only available for children **under 18** who have recently left education. Since Blake is **19 years old**, he doesn't qualify for the extension period (which is only for those under 18).

Also, since Blake left school "a while back" and isn't currently in any approved education or training, he unfortunately doesn't meet the eligibility criteria.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

**Summary so far:**

| Child | Age | Status |
|-------|-----|--------|
| Alex | 10 | ✅ Passes age check - continuing assessment |
| Charlie | 2 | ✅ Passes age check - continuing assessment |
| Blake | 19 | ❌ **Not eligible** - not in approved education/training |

---

I'm sorry to say that **Blake (19)** is not eligible for Child Benefit because he is over 16 and not currently in approved education or training. If Blake were to enrol in an approved course (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study, such as A-levels, T-levels, or NVQs Level 1-3), you could potentially claim for him again.

---

Now, let's continue the assessment for **Alex and Charlie**. 

Are either Alex or Charlie receiving any benefits in their own right (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither Alex nor Charlie are receiving any benefits themselves.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for confirming that. Let me continue the assessment for Alex and Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Since neither Alex nor Charlie are receiving benefits, they pass that check. 

**Next question for Alex and Charlie:**

Are either Alex or Charlie enrolled in an **apprenticeship**? (Given their ages of 10 and 2, I would assume not, but I need to confirm.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not - they're far too young for apprenticeships!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's what I thought! Let me continue the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now we need to check **your** (the claimant's) residency status.

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You only need to meet **one** of these criteria:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You are a resident of the UK
2. **Settled status** - You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** - You have pre-settled status and are working/earning above the National Insurance threshold
4. **Pre-settled status and seeking work** - You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** - You have pre-settled status and can support yourself financially (without claiming certain benefits)
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** - You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as a family member** - You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant abroad** - You're a Crown servant living abroad

Do you meet any of these criteria? For most people, option 1 (normally living in the UK) applies.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirement. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check your responsibility for Alex and Charlie.

**Are you responsible for Alex and Charlie?**

You meet this requirement if **any** of the following apply:

1. **Living with the child** - Alex and/or Charlie live with you
2. **Contributing to their upkeep** - You pay at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **Paying maintenance** - You make maintenance payments under a court order or agreement

Do either or both of these children live with you, or do you contribute to their upkeep in another way?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with me at home, but Charlie doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Let me note that for each child:

**For Alex (10):** Since Alex lives with you, you meet the responsibility requirement. ✅

**For Charlie (2):** Since Charlie doesn't live with you, I need to ask a follow-up question:

Do you **contribute to Charlie's upkeep** in any of the following ways?

- Paying for food, clothes, pocket money, housing, or presents
- Making maintenance payments under a court order or agreement
- Providing support that equals or exceeds the Child Benefit amount (either regularly or as a lump sum)

If you contribute financially to Charlie's care in any of these ways, you may still be considered responsible for them. Do you contribute to Charlie's upkeep?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not currently able to contribute anything financially towards Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for being honest about that. I'm sorry to say that this means you wouldn't be eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

**Update on Charlie (2):** ❌ **Not eligible**

To claim Child Benefit, you must be "responsible" for the child, which means either living with them OR contributing at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards their care. Since Charlie doesn't live with you and you're not currently contributing financially, you wouldn't be eligible to claim for Charlie.

However, **whoever Charlie lives with** (and is responsible for Charlie's care) may be able to claim Child Benefit for them instead.

---

Now let's continue the assessment for **Alex (10)**, who lives with you. Let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (10):**

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or planning to claim?

- **No** - No one else is claiming for Alex
- **Yes, but we've agreed I should claim** - Someone else was claiming, but they've agreed to transfer the claim to you
- **Yes, and it's disputed** - There's a disagreement about who should claim

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, no one else is claiming for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you! Let me continue with the assessment for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (10):**

I need to check Alex's current living situation. Is Alex:

1. **In hospital** - Currently staying in hospital
2. **In care** - Currently in local authority care
3. **Fostered** - Currently in a fostering arrangement
4. **Living normally at home** - Living with you at home in a normal arrangement

Which of these best describes Alex's situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in care for a couple of months, but he does live with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. It sounds like Alex may be in a shared care arrangement where he's in local authority care but also spends time living with you. Let me check the specific rules around this.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex has been in care for a couple of months (which is more than 8 weeks), there are specific rules that apply.

**For Alex (10):**

Even though Alex has been in care for more than 8 weeks, you can still be eligible for Child Benefit if Alex spends enough time at home with you. Does Alex:

- Spend **at least 24 hours per week** at home with you, **OR**
- Spend **at least 2 consecutive nights per week** at home with you, **OR**
- Spend **at least 7 consecutive days in a row** at home with you?

If any of these apply, you may still be eligible. Does Alex spend this amount of time at home with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex does spend time at home with me regularly.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's good to hear! As long as Alex spends at least 24 hours per week at home with you (or 2 consecutive nights per week, or 7+ consecutive days), you can still qualify for Child Benefit despite the care arrangement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**One more question for Alex:**

Is the local council currently paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance (for the time Alex is in care)?

- **No** - The council is NOT paying for Alex's accommodation/maintenance
- **Yes** - The council IS paying for Alex's accommodation/maintenance

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Let me confirm Alex's eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great news! I now have all the information I need to provide you with the eligibility outcome for all three children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 📋 Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Final Results

| Child | Age | Eligible? | Reason |
|-------|-----|-----------|--------|
| **Alex** | 10 | ✅ **YES** | Meets all requirements |
| **Blake** | 19 | ❌ **NO** | Not in approved education/training |
| **Charlie** | 2 | ❌ **NO** | You are not responsible for Charlie |

---

### ✅ Alex (10) - ELIGIBLE

Great news! You can claim Child Benefit for Alex. Even though Alex has been in care, because he spends regular time at home with you and the council isn't paying for his accommodation or maintenance, you meet all the requirements.

**Next Steps for Alex:**
1. Apply online or by post using form CH2
2. Provide proof of identity for yourself and Alex
3. Provide Alex's birth certificate or adoption certificate
4. Payments are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

⚠️ **Note:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge. You can still claim, but some or all of the benefit may need to be repaid through a tax charge.

---

### ❌ Blake (19) - NOT ELIGIBLE

Blake doesn't qualify because he's over 16 and not in approved education or training. However, if Blake decides to enrol in an approved course (e.g., A-levels, T-levels, NVQs Level 1-3, or BTECs) with at least 12 hours per week of supervised study, you could potentially claim for him again.

---

### ❌ Charlie (2) - NOT ELIGIBLE

You can't claim for Charlie because you don't live with Charlie and aren't contributing financially to their care. However, **whoever Charlie lives with** (and is responsible for Charlie's care) may be able to claim Child Benefit for Charlie instead.

---

For more information or to apply, visit: **https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim**

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex (10 years old) meets all the eligibility requirements: (1) Under 16 years old - passes age check, (2) Not working 24+ hours per week, (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) Claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), (6) Claimant is responsible for Alex as Alex lives with them, (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Although Alex has been in care for more than 8 weeks, Alex spends regular time at home with the claimant, and (9) The local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (19 years old) is not eligible because he is over 16 and not currently in approved education or training. For 16-19 year olds, Child Benefit can only continue if they are enrolled in approved education or training (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study). Since Blake finished school 'a while back' and is not in any education or training, he does not meet the qualifying child criteria.

Charlie (2 years old) is not eligible because the claimant is not considered 'responsible' for Charlie. Charlie does not live with the claimant, and the claimant is not currently contributing financially towards Charlie's upkeep. To be eligible, the claimant must either live with the child OR contribute at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care.
arguments.overall_reasoning: Of the three children, only Alex qualifies for Child Benefit. The claimant lives in the UK and meets all residency requirements. For Alex, despite being in a care arrangement, the child spends regular time at home and the council is not paying for accommodation/maintenance, so eligibility is maintained. Blake is too old (19) and not in approved education/training. Charlie does not live with the claimant and the claimant does not contribute to Charlie's upkeep, so the responsibility requirement is not met.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.reason: No qualifying child under 16, or under 20 in approved education/training
response.guidance: Child must be under 16, or aged 16-19 and in approved education or training. If child just turned 20, benefit stops. If 16-17 year old leaves education, benefit can continue for 20 weeks if they register with careers service or armed services.

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.guidance: You must be responsible for the child, which means either living with them OR paying at least the Child Benefit amount towards their care (food, clothes, housing, pocket money, etc.). This can include maintenance payments under court order.

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR do they spend at least 24 hours a week at home?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 8 weeks in care. Exception if child spends 24+ hours/week at home. Can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights/week or 7+ days in a row at home.

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (10 years old) meets all the eligibility requirements: (1) Under 16 years old - passes age check, (2) Not working 24+ hours per week, (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) Claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), (6) Claimant is responsible for Alex as Alex lives with them, (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Although Alex has been in care for more than 8 weeks, Alex spends regular time at home with the claimant, and (9) The local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (19 years old) is not eligible because he is over 16 and not currently in approved education or training. For 16-19 year olds, Child Benefit can only continue if they are enrolled in approved education or training (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study). Since Blake finished school 'a while back' and is not in any education or training, he does not meet the qualifying child criteria.

Charlie (2 years old) is not eligible because the claimant is not considered 'responsible' for Charlie. Charlie does not live with the claimant, and the claimant is not currently contributing financially towards Charlie's upkeep. To be eligible, the claimant must either live with the child OR contribute at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care.

response.overall_reasoning: Of the three children, only Alex qualifies for Child Benefit. The claimant lives in the UK and meets all residency requirements. For Alex, despite being in a care arrangement, the child spends regular time at home and the council is not paying for accommodation/maintenance, so eligibility is maintained. Blake is too old (19) and not in approved education/training. Charlie does not live with the claimant and the claimant does not contribute to Charlie's upkeep, so the responsibility requirement is not met.
